revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | width = 250px | caption = Nakayama Shrine | type = | dedication = Reitaisai (August 4th) | founded = 707 | closed = | founder = | priest = | reisai = Otauesai (29 April) | honden = Nakayma-zukuri | address = 695 Ixhinomiya, [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] | latd = 35| latm = 6| lats = 0.78| latNS = N | longd=133| longm=59| longs=40.53| longEW= E | iso_region = JP | coordinates_display = title | location_map = Japan | map_caption =Location within Japan | website = }} '''Nakayama Shrine''' (中山神社, ''Nakayama Jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine in [[Tsuyama, Okayama]] Prefecture, Japan. The main [[kami]] enshrined at Nakayama Jinja is Kagamitsukuri no kami (鏡作神), the god of mirror-making. It was formerly a national shrine of the second rank (官幣社, ''kanpeisha'') in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was established in 707. It was the [[ichinomiya]] of the former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 29. ==External link== {{Commons|Category:Nakayama-jinja}} *[http://www.okayama-kanko.jp/modules/kankouinfo/pub_kihon_detail.php?sel_id=1168&sel_data_kbn=0 Local tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:707 establishments]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: expand
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen"{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} {{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Tsuyama, Okayama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen"{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} {{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen"{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} {{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]]

During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]]

{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]]

After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]]

During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]]

According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category|Nakayama-jinja}} *[https://www.okayama-jinjacho.or.jp/search/16978/ Okayama Jinjacho home page] *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Tsuyama]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Mimasaka Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} revision.comment: bumping wikipedia derived articles
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} revision.text:
{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category:Pages and https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} revision.text:
{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from *[https://www.city.tsuyama.lg.jp/life/index2.php?id=3451#ﾅｶﾔﾏｼﾞﾝｼﾞｬ Tsuyama City official homepage] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Nakayama {{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:kk]] 中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shintō shrine in Tsuyama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nakayama Shrine
中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mimasaka Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 29. Although the ''[[kanji]]'' of the shrine's name is now pronounced "Nakayama", in the past the shrine was often referred to by its alternative pronunciation "Chuzen Jinja" or "Chuzen Dai-Gongen".{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nakayama Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Kagamitsukuri-no-kami'''| 鏡作神|}}, god of mirrors * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-Nukado-no-kami'''| 天糠戸神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto]]'''| 石凝姥命 |}}, goddess of mirrors and metal casting ==History== The origins of Nakayama Jinja are uncertain. According to the shrine's undocumented legend, it was founded in 707 AD. There is another theory that the shrine was built when Mimasaka Province separated from [[Bizen Province]] in 703. The earliest time the shrine appears in documentary evidence is in an entry dated 860 in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. Per the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD, the shrine is listed as the only {{nihongo|''Myōjin taisha''|名神大社||}} in Mimasaka Province, and by the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. During the [[Sengoku period]], in 1533, the shrine was burned down by [[Amago Haruhisa]] during his invasion of the province, and reconstructed by Haruhisa himself in 1559. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was designated as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1559 as a donation by Amago Haruhisa. It is a three by three bay hall in a unique shrine architecture commonly known as "Nakayama-zukuri" which is found only in this area of Japan. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3024|title=中山神社本殿|trans-title=Nakayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Nakayama-jinja haiden.JPG|''Haiden'' File:Nakayama-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kagura stage File:Nakayama-jinja shinmon-2.JPG|Gate（Tsuyama City Tangible Cultural Property） File:Nakayama-jinja keyaki.JPG|Keyaki Tree（Tsuyama City Natural Monument） File:Nakayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Shrine]][[Category:kk]] 中山神社 | image = Nakayama Jinja 09.JPG | caption = Haiden and Honden of Nakayama Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|06|0.78|N|133|59|40.53|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nakayama Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kagamitsukuri-no-kami | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 695 Ichinomiya, Tsuyama-shi, Okayama-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = April 29 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nakayama Jinja'''|中山神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tsuyama, Okayama|Tsuyama]] in [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]].
